quality.rationale: 概念存在性证明结合度规拼接技术与能动张量重构逻辑链条完整

origin.question:
[文件 1: 1_s13_13.8.md]

Problem 13.8. Is it possible to have a solution of the Einstein field equations in which space is empty to the past of some surface of constant time $t = 0$ , but in which there is a nonvanishing $T_{\mu \nu}$ to the future of this surface?

## Solution

Solution 13.8. Yes. Write down a metric $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}$ such that $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu} = \eta_{\mu \nu}$ for $t < 0$ (this is clearly empty space) and $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu} =$ (arbitrary functions of your choice) for $t \geq 0$ . The only constraint on the arbitrary functions is that they be twice differentiable and match smoothly to the flat-space values at the $t = 0$ surface. Now from this metric $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}$ we can calculate the Ricci tensor and scalar and we can define a tensor $\mathbf{T}_{\mu \nu} \equiv (8\pi)^{-1}(\mathbf{R}_{\mu \nu} - \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}\mathbf{R})$ . This tensor is obviously symmetric, and by the contracted Bianchi identities is divergenceless. If we fill spacetime with a stress-energy equal to this, then $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}$ is a solution of the Einstein field equations. (However, for a physically meaningful solution there must be further requirements for $\mathbf{T}_{\mu \nu}$ , e.g. energy density should be everywhere nonnegative. This will not in general be true for the procedure above.)

Write down a metric $\mathbf{g}_{\\mu \\nu}$ such that $\mathbf{g}_{\\mu \\nu} = \\eta_{\\mu \\nu}$ for $t < 0$ (this is clearly empty space) and $\mathbf{g}_{\\mu \\nu} =$ (arbitrary functions of your choice) for $t \\geq 0$ . The only constraint on the arbitrary functions is that they be twice differentiable and match smoothly to the flat-space values at the $t = 0$ surface. Now from this metric $\mathbf{g}_{\\mu \\nu}$ we can calculate the Ricci tensor and scalar and we can define a tensor $\mathbf{T}_{\\mu \\nu} \\equiv (8\\pi)^{-1}(\\mathbf{R}_{\\mu \\nu} - \\frac{1}{2}\\mathbf{g}_{\\mu \\nu}\\mathbf{R})$ . This tensor is obviously symmetric, and by the contracted Bianchi identities is divergenceless. If we fill spacetime with a stress-energy equal to this, then $\mathbf{g}_{\\mu \\nu}$ is a solution of the Einstein field equations. (However, for a physically meaningful solution there must be further requirements for $\mathbf{T}_{\\mu \\nu}$ , e.g. energy density should be everywhere nonnegative. This will not in general be true for the procedure above.)
